2022-08-28

What is org.greenrobot.greendao.annotation.Generated?

org.greenrobot.greendao.annotation.Generated is an annotation type defined in the GreenDao library, which was designed to simplify database usage in Android applications. It is used to mark a class, field, or method as generated by the library, thus allowing the code to be reused in different projects, while ensuring that changes made to the original source are not overwritten.

Code Examples

Here is a simple example of using the Generated annotation: 


          
           
@Generated
public class User {
   private int id;
   private String name;
   // Getters and setters
}

This tells the library that this class was generated, so it will not overwrite any changes made to the source code. This annotation can also be used to indicate that a specific field or method was also generated:


          
           
@Generated
public class User {
   @Generated
   private int id;
   @Generated
   private String name;
 
   @Generated
   public void setId(int id) {
      this.id = id;
   }
   // etc.
}

In this example, the annotation is added to both the fields and the method, indicating that these elements were generated by the library. This ensures that any external changes to the code will not be overwritten.

Using the org.greenrobot.greendao.annotation.Generated Annotation with Other Annotations

The Generated annotation can also be used alongside other annotations. For example, here is a class with both the Generated and Entity annotations:


          
           
@Generated
@Entity
public class User {
   @Generated
   @Id
   private int id;
   ...
}

In this case, both the @Generated and @Entity annotations will be used in order to indicate that the class was generated by the library and that it should be treated as an entity class by the library.

Conclusion

The org.greenrobot.greendao.annotation.Generated annotation is useful for indicating that a class, field, or method was generated by the GreenDao library. It can be used to prevent external changes from being overwritten, and can also be used in conjunction with other annotations, such as @Entity.
